does: This paper develops an event-grounded interpretability pipeline for Vision-Language-Action robot policies using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs). Rather than analyzing latent features in isolation, the method anchors SAE feature analysis to behaviorally salient events by clustering end-effector keyframes within tasks using visual, state, and temporal cues. These clusters link individual SAE features to specific behavioral outcomes and optionally to semantic meanings via VLM annotations. The authors validate their approach across two simulation architectures and real robot experiments, demonstrating that event-grounded feature ranking produces the strongest causal effects on the OpenVLA policy and transfers to continuous action policies like π₀.₅, establishing SAEs as interpretable but imperfect tools for understanding VLA representations.
connects: This work extends mechanistic interpretability methods originally developed for language models and vision-language models to the distinct domain of robot control policies. While SAEs have proven effective for interpreting neural network features in LLMs and VLMs by identifying sparse, interpretable directions, their application to VLAs presents unique challenges: robot actions are continuous and high-dimensional rather than discrete tokens, and policy analysis traditionally relied on expensive closed-loop rollouts rather than token-level interventions. The paper responds to this gap by combining SAE-based feature analysis with behavioral grounding techniques from robotics, drawing on prior work in both mechanistic interpretability and VLA policy understanding to create a domain-adapted analysis framework.
enables: This interpretability pipeline opens pathways for more systematic analysis and debugging of robot policies by connecting latent representations to observable behaviors. The event-grounded approach enables future work on causal intervention in VLAs beyond expensive rollouts, potentially allowing offline feature manipulation and policy improvement. The framework could support developing better alignment techniques for robot policies by making it easier to identify which features drive undesired behaviors. Additionally, the behavioral grounding method could generalize to other continuous-action domains and inform the design of more interpretable architectures. The finding that SAEs transfer across policy architectures suggests a foundation for building more transparent and trustworthy autonomous systems in robotics.
